words: Oh my father was a New York Irishman. You never were anything but a democrat.

words: You were that til the New Deal came along, then! he didn't like that.

speaker: SS
words: He didn't like the New Deal?

speaker: CM
words: Oh no. Didn't like it at all, didn't like what was going on. But up til then

speaker: CM
words: he was a good Democrat, even probably, I don't know that he always voted in

speaker: CM
words: eastern Oregon,'cause sometimes it was much more economic for them if the

speaker: CM
words: Republicans won. That was ahard thing, hard decision then. But he didn't

speaker: CM
words: like the New Deal and his sister had paid him a visit and they hadn't seen

speaker: CM
words: each other for many, many years. And dad could imitate Roosevelt perfectly.

speaker: CM
words: And he did something, and she was furious ! She liked Roosevelt. I think if

speaker: CM
words: there had been a train out here at ten o'clock at night she'd have taken it.

speaker: CM
words: And mother and I finally saved the day by laughing at him, 'cause it really

speaker: CM
words: was funny. Brother and sister after all this time, just quarreling so. And

speaker: CM
words: she says,"You're nothing but a Republican!" And no word ever sounded worse

speaker: CM
words: than the way she said Republican. Well, he wasn't a Roosevelt Democrat, they

speaker: CM
words: wouldn't admit to being Republican, because they were Democrats. Those New

speaker: CM
words: York Irish were Democrats. They were democrats before they left the old

speaker: CM
words: country, almost. It was in the family to be Democrats. It was in my mother's

speaker: CM
words: idea that you were always the underdog. She always supported the underdog.

speaker: CM
words: The party that was out. Now of course I know she voted for French

speaker: CM
words: who was Republican. If she voted. She couldn't vote up there, she kept her

speaker: CM
words: vote down here. But she would have voted for French, of course. I mean, but

speaker: CM
words: she'd always vote for the one that was out. That was a small party as a rule.

speaker: CM
words: Usually speaking, she would support that. But I think they.had supported Bryant.

speaker: CM
words: And I don't know, I think my grandfather, who read, my mother's father, who

speaker: CM
words: read all the papers very thoroughly, kept up. And he was for the

speaker: CM
words: underdog. For a European, coming to this country, he learned the language

speaker: CM
words: by reading the paper. He said he made a point to read every day. The paper.

speaker: CM
words: If he didn't know a word he called it Jerusalem. And to begin with, there

speaker: CM
words: were quite a few Jerusalems. But he got, and he spoke with no accent, except

speaker: CM
words: that he said he was a Eur-op-ean. And my father had no Irish accent.

speaker: SS
words: Your father had come from Ireland?

speaker: CM
words: No, his folks had, but lots of that generation kept an accent, but he didn't.

speaker: CM
words: He didn't think that was right at all. He despised people that kept an accent.

speaker: CM
words: Why it was ridiculous, you know. To him, oh no, no accent..It wasn't til I

speaker: CM
words: was east with him, I know that my, I met, my generation, which was third generation

speaker: CM
words: Irish, and some of them spoke with an accent. Our faether. You know. What?

speaker: CM
words: What are they saying almost? It just seemed funny to me, because some do lose

speaker: CM
words: it and some just, I think, I couldn't understand. There was one bunch of kids

speaker: CM
words: and two boys and their mother was a graduate of the university there in Syracuse.

speaker: CM
words: Father was successful businessman."Yes faether." What in the world why they

speaker: CM
words: didn't get over the accent. Course, it's kind of fun to put it on, but to

speaker: CM
words: have it,its a thing that you can't drop, its kind of like a Southern accent.

speaker: CM
words: Why keep it.

speaker: SS
words: Please tell me the story of Jake Rosenstein. He started out as a peddler?

speaker: CM
words: Started out as a padller and carried a pack on his back as a peddler. That

speaker: CM
words: was his begining. And as he would have said, he was a poor Jew. His sister

speaker: CM
words: had married a wealthier Jew. And lived in Oakland and he had a very wealthy

speaker: CM
words: brother in Roseburg. And they financed my grandfather in business. But

speaker: CM
words: Jake of course prospered in the end. He was very successful in Genesee.

speaker: SS
words: It was the story you told me, he went to a way station?

speaker: CM
words: yes, there terrible way stations sometimes where you had to stay,

speaker: CM
words: and this one was one of those places and it was no place for miles. And he

speaker: CM
words: had come inpprobably, still with his pack, he was peddling, Jew, and they

speaker: CM
words: charged abig price for the room  You probably had to take the two

speaker: CM
words: together. And Ithink the meal costed him about a dollar, and you could get

speaker: CM
words: a good dinner for aquarter. And avery fine dinner for fifty eents. And there

speaker: CM
words: wasr.nothing but ham and mustard, he said. And he told the man,"I don't eat

speaker: CM
words: ham. I'm Jewish." And he said,"Well help yourself to the mustard." That was

speaker: CM
words: it. I know he told my grandfather the t, and they had had these various experiences

speaker: CM
words: in travelling through where they had had hardships.

speaker: SS
words: Your grandfather?

speaker: CM
words: Oh yes. And anybody in the west, my father and my mother had, at various times.

speaker: CM
words: You might travel from one place to another and you see, you travel by stagecoach,

speaker: CM
words: and it was slow travel. And one place you stopped where they fed them

speaker: CM
words: and changed horses, and they might be very nice. You might have a good bed,

speaker: CM
words: you might have warm and good food. The next place, just terrible. My mother,

speaker: CM
words: in eastern Oregon, when she was saying about the company, telephone company,

speaker: CM
words: one place.they got in, she said, about four or five, in the morning. It was

speaker: CM
words: very cold. They'd ridden all night in the stagecach, no top on it, in the

speaker: CM
words: rain and the blanket that they had to cover themself was so heavy, almost

speaker: CM
words: let it slide off, and it was wet and she was so miserable, and the mud had

speaker: CM
words: gotten in her face and she was in a mess. And she was so cold that she said,

speaker: CM
words: "I don't want a room, I just want to get warm by the stove."Cause they would

speaker: CM
words: be leaving, and they said, we put out the fire, you have to take a robe. Well

speaker: CM
words: it didn't make any difference in money to her, because the company was paying

speaker: CM
words: for it.

speaker: CM
words: She had to take a room. And so she said she didn't undaess at all

speaker: CM
words: and it was a good thing,there were kind of horse blankets on the bed. And

speaker: CM
words: then the next morning there were some badly burned biscuits and that was all

speaker: CM
words: and it was a dollar for the meal. And she said, there was some side pork,

speaker: CM
words: it was something you couldn't eat. Nothing else. It was just terrible. Coffee

speaker: CM
words: that was undrinkable. And my father, if they compared notes, had been going

speaker: CM
words: through on the same route. And I think either before or just after. And he

speaker: CM
words: heard,they pulled the same stunt on him and he heard the girls snickering

speaker: CM
words: about, said wonder what he'd think if he knew the man before him had smallpox

speaker: CM
words: in there. But neither one of em caught smallpox, and they weren't vacinated for

speaker: CM
words: it, ei4h er, but I think it was 'cause they didn't undress. They were so cold,

speaker: CM
words: so miserable that they just didn't take off their clothes and the horse blankets,

speaker: CM
words: they weren't very attractive. And maybe they had something else around them

speaker: CM
words: too. Everything that their own personal things and they evidently didn't catch

speaker: CM
words: it, but then the next place might be very nice, and feed you well. But you

speaker: CM
words: were at the mercy, in travelling, of these way stations. And sometimes they

speaker: CM
words: were pretty awful. And it was slow travel. The stagecoach. Horse and buggy

speaker: CM
words: and mud and bad weather and over almost nonexistent mountain roads. Two hundred

speaker: CM
words: miles to your nearest depot. Freight train. It was rugged in that...
